behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: The exponential backoff calculation is deterministic library logic with no external dependencies. Mocking the HTTP layer to return 429 responses and using fake timers (Jest fake timers), we can assert the retry intervals follow the pattern (100ms → 200ms → 400ms) and respect the maximum cap. Failing to double correctly or exceeding the max would be caught immediately.
behaviors.test_description: Mock HTTP client to return 429 on every call. Use fake timers to avoid slow tests. Trigger a call, capture the retry attempts, and verify that intervals between retries double each time and never exceed the configured maximum (e.g., 10 seconds).

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Response parsing and Retry-After extraction are deterministic operations on local data. Mock HTTP to return 429 with headers (e.g., `Retry-After: 60`). Assert the library extracts the correct wait time without calling real APIs.
behaviors.test_description: Mock HTTP to return 429 status with `Retry-After: 60` header. Call the API. Verify the library extracts 60 seconds as the wait time, not a default or incorrect value. Test edge cases: missing header, malformed header, server-side override logic.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Resend and result-return logic is local to the library. Mock HTTP to return 429 on the first call, 200 on the second. Verify the library resends the same request and returns the successful response to the caller without exposing retry details.
behaviors.test_description: Mock HTTP to return 429 on first call, 200 with response data on second. Call the API with specific request parameters. Verify HTTP is called exactly twice (same request body both times), and the caller receives the 200 response data without seeing the 429 error.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Error handling is pure library logic. Mock HTTP to always return 429, exhaust the retry limit, and verify the specific typed error is thrown (not a generic Error or HTTPError).
behaviors.test_description: Mock HTTP to always return 429. Configure max retries (e.g., 3). Call the API. Assert that `QuotaExhaustedError` (not a generic error) is thrown, with an appropriate error message.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: The termination property of the retry loop is a logical invariant. Mock HTTP to always return 429. Run with a test timeout (e.g., Jest 5s default). If the retry loop spins indefinitely, the timeout will trigger, catching the infinite-loop bug.
behaviors.test_description: Mock HTTP to always return 429. Call the API with a test timeout (5 seconds). Verify the call completes and returns `QuotaExhaustedError` before timeout. This catches infinite loops in the retry logic.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Quota sharing is an in-memory state invariant. In JavaScript (single-threaded), concurrent async calls to the same client instance share memory. Mock HTTP to limit total allowed requests (e.g., first 5 return 200, rest return 429). If quota is not shared (e.g., per-call buckets), more calls would succeed than the limit allows.
behaviors.test_description: Create one client instance with quota limit 5. Use Promise.all to issue 10 concurrent calls. Mock HTTP to succeed for first 5 requests, then fail with 429 for rest. Verify exactly 5 succeed; if quota is not shared, more would succeed, failing the test.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Request ordering depends on the queue structure within the library. Mock HTTP to return 429 on first attempt, then 200 on retry. Use fake timers to control when retries happen. Track the sequence of HTTP calls to verify FIFO order. Non-FIFO queue implementations (heap, priority queue, LIFO) would produce a different order and fail the test.
behaviors.test_description: Issue 3 concurrent calls (A, B, C) that all receive 429. Use fake timers to advance time and trigger retries. Mock HTTP on retry to return 200. Track the order of HTTP calls and verify retries are dispatched in order A→B→C (submission order), not C→B→A or any other permutation.
